mappings.rationaleAI: Catherine II's westernization, partitions of Poland, successful wars against the Ottoman Empire, and Enlightenment patronage directly address further westernization and expansion under Catherine II (A.3.d) and education and social change in the 18th century (A.3.e).

Suleiman I's conflicts with Habsburgs in Hungary, annexation of Iraq, and legal codification directly address the Ottoman Empire's consolidation under Bayezid II and Suleyman's campaigns (B.1.a.i) and classical Ottoman society and administration (B.1.a.ii).

The Ottoman Empire from 1481 through Tanzimat reforms, the millet system, and military decline directly addresses the Ottoman Empire as the dominant power (B.1.a), decline from 1566 (B.1.b), and imperial disintegration in the 18th century (B.1.c).
The early modern period's coverage of the Ottoman Empire, Safavid Iran, and European expansion provides broad context for the Christian states of eastern Europe (A) and Islamic states (B).

Russia's history from Muscovite principality through the Tsardom and Russian Empire directly addresses the emergence of the Russian Empire (A.3), including Muscovite control (A.3.a), Romanov Muscovy (A.3.b), Peter the Great's westernization (A.3.c), and Catherine the Great's expansion (A.3.d).
Iran's Safavid dynasty as the Shi'ite state religion and subsequent Zand and Qajar dynasties directly address Iran from c. 1500 to 1779 (B.2.a).

Poland's Golden Age, Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, three partitions, and loss of statehood directly address Poland-Lithuania from 1492 to 1795 (A.1), including the Golden Age (A.1.a), royal republic (A.1.b), wars and disintegration (A.1.c), and the partitions (A.1.d).
The Mughal Empire's Safavid and Ottoman diplomatic relations relate to the Safavid dynasty's conflict with the Turks and possession of western Afghanistan (B.2.a.i).
The Reformation's effects in Eastern Europe relate to the effects of the Renaissance and Reformation in Poland-Lithuania (A.1.a.ii).
The Enlightenment's impact on Russian education and social change relates to the impact of the Enlightenment on 18th-century Russia (A.3.e).
The Byzantine Empire's fall to the Ottomans in 1453 provides essential context for the Ottoman Empire as the dominant power of Southwest Asia and southeastern Europe (B.1.a).
The Holy Roman Empire's Habsburg dynasty relates to Hungary's partition between Ottoman and Habsburg sectors (A.2).
The Renaissance's spread to Poland relates to the effects of the Renaissance in Poland-Lithuania's domestic developments (A.1.a.ii).
The Islamic Golden Age's intellectual traditions provide context for classical Ottoman society including religious and civil law (B.1.a.ii).
The Mongol Empire's legacy in Russian state formation provides context for the emergence of the Russian Empire from Muscovite control (A.3.a).
